\title{Meet EMMA: Your Emergency Medicine Mentoring Agent}

%%
%% By default, the full list of authors will be used in the page
%% headers. Often, this list is too long, and will overlap
%% other information printed in the page headers. This command allows
%% the author to define a more concise list
%% of authors' names for this purpose.
\renewcommand{\shortauthors}{Dutta}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Medical licensing examinations demand synthesis of clinical reasoning across dozens
of specialties under high-stakes conditions. This paper presents EMMA (Emergency
Medicine Mentoring Agent), a conversational study agent that grounds responses in
passages from eighteen standard medical textbooks via a five-component machine
learning pipeline: biomedical NER for clinical entity extraction, FAISS-based dense
retrieval over 36,723 textbook chunks, TF-IDF + LinearSVC specialty classification,
BERTopic clustering for fine-grained topic routing, and a collaborative filtering
recommender. A six-run ablation grid varying embedding model, LLM, and RAG
condition shows that textbook-grounded RAG delivers up to an 11 percentage-point
accuracy gain on MedQA under the right embedding--LLM pairing, while degrading
performance under others. These findings suggest that RAG benefit is highly
sensitive to semantic alignment between the embedding space and retrieval corpus.
\end{abstract}
